parts.content: Describe the movement patterns and muscle contractions involved in a soccer kick.
parts.markscheme:
In a soccer kick:

- The quadriceps (front of thigh) undergo concentric (shortening) contraction to extend/straighten the leg <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The hip flexors (iliopsoas) contract concentrically to swing the leg forward <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- The hamstrings (back of thigh) perform eccentric (lengthening) contraction to control/decelerate the movement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The gluteus maximus (buttocks) contracts to stabilize the hip joint <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyze the role of biomechanics in improving balance and stability in a gymnast’s handstand.
parts.markscheme:
- Gymnast's body aligns vertically over the base (hands), with the center of mass directly above for stability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Muscle activation in the shoulders, core, and arms counteracts gravity, maintaining balance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Small adjustments in hand pressure and shoulder position help the gymnast stay stable, illustrating how biomechanics improve control in balance-focused movements <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the significance of the center of gravity in a high jumper’s takeoff and flight phases.
parts.markscheme:
- During takeoff, the high jumper's center of gravity moves upward, generating lift. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The goal is to position the body so the center of gravity clears the bar efficiently, which helps minimize energy use. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the flight phase, the jumper arches their body (Fosbury Flop technique) to keep the center of gravity below the bar, <marks>1 mark</marks>
- allowing for a successful clearance with minimal height. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the role of antagonistic muscle pairs, using the example of the biceps curl.
parts.markscheme:
- Antagonistic muscles act in opposition at a joint to control movement. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- During the lifting phase (elbow flexion), the biceps is the agonist and contracts (concentric) to flex the elbow. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- During the lifting phase, the triceps acts as the antagonist and relaxes/lengthens to allow the movement. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- During the lowering phase, the biceps lengthens under tension (eccentric contraction) to control the descent. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- During elbow extension/lowering, the triceps may contract as the agonist to extend the elbow. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: What is meant by the term “center of mass” and how does it affect a gymnast’s balance?
parts.markscheme:
- Centre of mass is the point at which the body’s mass can be considered to be concentrated/act. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Balance/stability is maintained when the vertical projection of the centre of mass stays within the base of support. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A lower centre of mass increases stability (easier to keep the projection within the base of support). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shifting the centre of mass closer to the edge/outside the base of support reduces stability and increases the likelihood of losing balance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how joint stability is maintained during a lunge exercise.
parts.markscheme:
- Co-contraction/activation of muscles around the hip, knee and ankle stabilises the joints and maintains alignment during the lunge. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Core/trunk activation helps maintain pelvic and spinal alignment, supporting lower-limb joint stability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hip abductors/external rotators (eg gluteus medius) help prevent knee valgus/medial collapse. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: What is the role of the patella in knee extension?
parts.markscheme:
- The patella increases the quadriceps’ moment arm/mechanical advantage, increasing knee extension torque. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It redirects the line of pull of the quadriceps tendon to make extension more effective. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:
- Defines a lever as a rigid body/structure that rotates about a fulcrum/axis (pivot) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Includes that an effort (force) acts to move a load (resistance) about the fulcrum/axis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies first-class lever: fulcrum between effort and load (E–F–L or L–F–E) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Gives a correct first-class example (e.g., neck extension at the atlanto-occipital joint when lifting the head; triceps extension at the elbow) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies second-class lever: load between fulcrum and effort (F–L–E) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Gives a correct second-class example (e.g., plantar flexion when standing on tiptoes; push-up at the toes) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies third-class lever: effort between fulcrum and load (F–E–L) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the difference between flexion and extension, providing an example in the context of running.

- Extension: straightening movement that increases the angle at a joint <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Define torque and explain its role in performing a golf swing.
parts.markscheme:
- Defines torque as the turning effect (moment) of a force about an axis/pivot <marks>1 mark</marks>
- States torque depends on force and perpendicular distance (moment arm) from the axis (e.g., $\tau = F \times d_\perp$ / $\tau = rF\sin\theta$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that torso/hip and shoulder rotation generates torque about relevant axes, producing angular acceleration of the body/club <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links greater torque to increased angular velocity/club head speed and therefore greater ball speed/distance (if contact is effective) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links controlled application/timing of torque to maintaining swing plane/face orientation for accuracy/consistency <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: What is a concentric muscle contraction? Provide an example in the context of a squat.

parts.content: Describe the role of angular motion in the spinning of sports equipment (for example, a ball), with reference to the soccer free kick in the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- Angular motion defined as rotation of an object about an axis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spinning equipment/ball exhibits angular motion that can influence its flight path/trajectory (e.g., via the Magnus effect) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spin can help stabilize flight via gyroscopic effects (maintains orientation/reduces wobble) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Direction/type of spin (e.g., topspin/backspin/sidespin) affects the amount/direction of curve and/or subsequent bounce behaviour <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (any may be credited):

- Credit other appropriate examples (tennis, cricket, golf) linked to angular motion and control <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept discussion of the Magnus effect or control in sports like tennis.</note>

parts.content: Explain how linear and angular momentum interact during a complex movement such as a backflip, with reference to the gymnast in the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- Linear momentum defined as $p=mv$ (mass $\times$ linear velocity) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Linear momentum is generated during take-off through impulse (force applied against the ground over time), contributing to upward/forward motion and time in the air <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Angular momentum is generated by torque at take-off, initiating rotation about an axis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conservation of angular momentum in flight: athlete changes body configuration (moment of inertia) to change angular velocity (tuck faster, extend slower) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (any may be credited):
- Link that insufficient linear momentum (insufficient vertical velocity) reduces flight time, making it harder to complete the required rotation even with high angular velocity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Link that insufficient angular momentum/torque at take-off leads to under-rotation even if jump height is adequate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reference that air resistance produces small external torques/forces but is usually minor compared with take-off effects for a gymnast <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using Table 1, explain how an athlete’s understanding of fluid mechanics can influence equipment choice and technique in cycling.
parts.markscheme:
- From Table 1, the aero tuck reduces drag compared with upright (38 N to 28 N), so technique/equipment that promotes a streamlined posture reduces drag and improves performance (less resistive force to overcome) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examples of equipment choices that help reduce drag: aero helmet, skin suit, deep-section wheels, smooth cable routing (any suitable example) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- From Table 1, lift is also reduced in the aero tuck (6 N to 3 N); athletes may adjust posture/positioning to manage lift as well as drag to improve stability/handling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use of drafting/positioning to reduce effective air resistance (reduced relative wind speed/pressure drag in the slipstream) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (any may be credited):
- Explain that reducing frontal surface area and/or promoting more laminar flow delays separation and can reduce pressure drag <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mention testing/feedback methods (e.g., wind tunnel, track aero testing) to optimise position and equipment choices <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Other acceptable answers include the use of wind tunnels for testing or adaptation to altitude/density conditions.</note>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.1.2 Planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which plane of motion a biceps curl occurs in, directly assessing knowledge of planes of movement (sagittal/frontal/transverse) and related human motion concepts.

options.markscheme: The frontal (coronal) plane involves primarily abduction and adduction movements, such as a jumping jack.
The sagittal plane involves flexion and extension movements. A biceps curl consists mainly of elbow flexion and extension, thus occurring primarily in this plane.
The transverse plane involves rotational movements, such as trunk rotation.
The horizontal plane is often used synonymously with the transverse plane and involves rotational movements, not the primary action in a biceps curl.

options.markscheme: Power is primarily generated during the forward swing/acceleration phase before ball contact, not during the follow-through.

Ball trajectory is mainly determined at (and immediately before) ball contact by foot orientation and point of contact; follow-through occurs after contact and is not the primary biomechanical purpose for trajectory control.

options.content: Reducing injury risk and ensuring smooth deceleration
options.markscheme: The follow-through phase occurs after ball contact. Its primary biomechanical purpose is to safely dissipate the kinetic energy of the limb by increasing the time and distance over which deceleration occurs, reducing peak forces on joints and muscles and minimizing injury risk.
Spin is primarily imparted at ball contact through foot-ball interaction and point of contact; follow-through happens after contact and is not the primary purpose for creating spin.
